turns.content: I'm used to using RefCell for interior mutability in shared references, but I noticed my colleague uses Cell<T> instead. What's the difference between these two approaches when dealing with copy types?

The key distinction is that RefCell requires a mutable reference to modify its contents, while Cell<T> only needs ownership of T and can be shared freely. Since you mentioned your colleague is working with copy types, Cell avoids the borrow checker complaints you see with RefCell. You'll get a compile error if you try to use RefCell on an immutable value without wrapping it in something mutable first.
So I should just swap out my RefCell for Cell everywhere since both handle interior mutability? My code compiles fine now with Cell instead of RefCell, right?

Not quite. While Cell works well for copy types where you only need to read or set values without borrowing the container itself, RefCell is still necessary when you need to borrow mutable state from within a shared reference context. For example, if your struct holds multiple interior-mutable fields and you're trying to update one while holding a reference to another field that also uses interior mutability, Cell alone won't help there.
Wait, so I can just use Cell for all my interior mutable values? I thought RefCell was only needed when the type itself needs to be mutable?

That's a common misunderstanding. You need RefCell when you're borrowing inside a context where the container is shared but you still want to mutate something within it using references, not just ownership swaps. Cell works best when you only ever swap or read values without needing temporary mutable borrows of the interior value itself.
Can you give me a concrete example where RefCell is absolutely required versus where Cell is enough? Like maybe something with Vec or String?

For a Vec, you could use Cell<Vec<T>> but it's awkward because you'd need to clone the entire vector every time you want to modify it. RefCell lets you borrow mutable access to individual elements without copying. With String since it's not Copy anyway, you wouldn't even consider using Cell or RefCell on the string itself—you'd wrap the interior state in one of them instead.
